mnemonics.title: Graphics Application Areas
mnemonics.content: CAD CAM CGI VR GIS = Creative Designers Make Incredible Virtual Geographic Systems. (CAD for design, CAM for manufacturing, CGI for movies, VR for simulation, GIS for mapping)

mnemonics.title: Rendering Pipeline Steps
mnemonics.content: V-R-F-P: Vertex processing → Rasterization → Fragment processing → Pixel output. Remember: Very Rigid Furry Penguins.

mnemonics.title: Graphics Hardware Components
mnemonics.content: GPU Frame Buffer Display = Great Pictures For Daily Use. GPU handles processing, Frame Buffer stores pixels, Display shows output.

mnemonics.title: Vector vs Raster
mnemonics.content: Vector = Scalable (no pixels), Raster = Fixed (pixels). Remember: Vector has Values, Raster has Rasterized pixels.

acronyms.usage: Used in engineering and architecture for precision design creation and modification
Uses graphics for process planning and control in manufacturing
Creating visual content for films, games, and simulations
Visual interface with windows, icons, menus for user interaction
Immersive 3D environment simulation for training and entertainment
Overlaying digital content on real-world views
Hardware component for parallel graphics rendering
Flat graphics with width and height only
Graphics with depth in addition to width and height

visualTips.visualization: Imagine layers of transparent sheets stacked - Z-buffer tracks which layer is closest to viewer at each point, showing only the visible surface.
Picture laser beams from camera bouncing off objects - trace each ray's path to see what colors reach the viewer's eye.
Think of gift-wrapping: the 2D texture is like wrapping paper that conforms to the 3D gift shape using UV coordinates.
Blending fence posts together at distance - smooth transitions where sharp edges appear stair-stepped.
Imagine a 3D mask made of triangular tiles - each triangle is a polygon, together they form the surface.

analogies.analogy: Like an artist's canvas - holds the complete picture as pixels until displayed on screen (the gallery wall).
GPU is like a team of specialized workers doing simple tasks simultaneously, while CPU is one expert doing complex tasks sequentially.
Vector is like a blueprint - mathematical instructions to build anywhere. Raster is like a photograph - fixed detail that degrades when enlarged.
Like manufacturing: raw materials (3D models) → assembly (rasterization) → painting (fragment processing) → packaging (frame buffer).
Like painting a fence - go horizontal line by line, deciding which sections need color within each pass.
